one_line_takeaway: In small (n=12 and n=15) IRB-approved user studies, the authors find that security/privacy education -- not ML background -- correlates with AML threat concern among practitioners, and that a CTF-based AML curriculum they built is well received in principle (n=12/15 solved a beginner poisoning-adjacent challenge, 0/15 solved the advanced one) but is not currently how even CTF-experienced practitioners learn about AML.
key_finding: Study 1: cybersecurity education/experience (Q1: alpha=0.017; Q4: alpha=0.035) and privacy education (Q6: alpha=0.001) were statistically correlated with AML concern, but ML background/experience was not (Q2 alpha=0.101, Q5 alpha=0.056) and privacy work-experience was not (Q3 alpha=0.06). None of the 12 participants cited CTF platforms as an AML learning source (H2 not supported), and 5/12 reported having personally encountered ML-related security vulnerabilities on the job; 68% felt security adoption impacts model accuracy, 16% cited time, 16% cited performance. Study 2: 12/15 participants solved Challenge 1 (LollyAI) but 0/15 solved Challenge 2 (poisoning chatbot); participants engaged far more with general cybersecurity CTF topics (e.g., Web Exploitation n=9, Cryptography n=9, Binary Exploitation n=6) than ML-specific ones (Adversarial ML n=1, Neural Networks n=1, out of n=15 on Table III). A Fisher's exact test found educational background correlated with preference for CTF as an AML learning method (alpha=0.006, supporting H1), but prior CTF usage history did not correlate with preference for CTF-based AML learning (alpha=0.6, H2 not supported) -- i.e., regular CTF users did not necessarily use CTFs for AML education specifically.
core_contribution: The paper reports two original user studies on Adversarial Machine Learning (AML) conducted by the authors: (1) a 12-participant online survey of industry professionals (recruited via WiCyS 2023 posters and a university alumni platform) examining how background in security/ML/privacy correlates with AML threat concern, CTF usage, and adoption factors for security practices; and (2) a 15-participant in-person study at Carnegie Mellon University evaluating two novel CTF challenges the authors built and deployed as local Flask web apps (LollyAI, a beginner NLP/logistic-regression 'adaptive retraining' challenge, and Bake-time Lolly Chatbot, an advanced NLTK/SVM-sigmoid-kernel challenge requiring a poisoning attack referencing Feature Collision and Convex Polytope concepts) as an educational tool for AML concepts. The paper's own contribution is the design, execution, and statistical analysis (Fisher's exact test) of these two studies plus a resulting set of recommendations for integrating AML into security education and industry practice.
threat_model: Not a technical threat model in the adversary-capability sense; the paper is a human-subjects education/awareness study. It surveys perceptions of AML attack categories (poisoning, evasion/adversarial-perturbation, model extraction/reverse-engineering) as reported by participants, and its CTF challenges implement a training-time data-poisoning scenario against a toy chatbot classifier as a pedagogical exercise, not as a novel attack technique.
limitations: The paper explicitly states its own limitations: Study 1's participant pool (n=12) is small and cannot generalize to the whole industry, and Study 2's participants were heavily skewed toward CMU alumni/students, introducing demographic bias that may limit applicability. Additional limitations evident from the text but not flagged by the authors: Study 2 had zero solvers for the advanced challenge, which limits what can be concluded about its educational effectiveness versus its difficulty calibration; sample sizes for sub-analyses (e.g., neurel-network security perception questions restricted to 8/12 participants) are very small for statistical claims; self-selected 'security/CTF enthusiast' recruitment in Study 2 may bias results toward participants already favorable to CTF-based learning.

quantitative_results: Study 1 (n=12): Fisher's exact test alpha values -- Q1&Q13 (security experience vs. concern) = 0.017 (significant); Q4&Q13 (security education vs. concern) = 0.035 (significant); Q6&Q13 (privacy education vs. concern) = 0.001 (significant); Q2&Q13 (ML experience vs. concern) = 0.101 (not significant); Q3&Q13 (privacy experience vs. concern) = 0.06 (not significant); Q5&Q13 (ML education vs. concern) = 0.056 (not significant)

Study 1: 7/8 participants with neural-network experience were familiar with CNNs but only 4/8 perceived CNN security issues; only 1/8 had GAN experience yet 4 identified GAN vulnerabilities; 5/8 believed data poisoning was a plausible attack, 2/8 cited adversarial input perturbation, 1/8 cited model extraction/reverse engineering
Study 1: 5/12 participants reported encountering security vulnerabilities in ML projects; 7/12 actively keep up with security trends; none of the 12 cited CTF platforms as an AML information source
Study 1: 68% felt security adoption most impacts accuracy, 16% time, 16% performance

Study 2 (n=15 recruited/selected): 11 Master's, 3 Bachelor's, 1 PhD; 10/15 Information Security specialty, 7/15 Networks, 6/15 Programming, 2/15 AI, 2/15 Data Science, 1/15 Psychology; 93% had prior programming experience; 93% had prior CTF experience (picoCTF 52%, HackTheBox 21%, TryHackMe 21%, VulnHub 4%)

Study 2 Table III (n=15): general-security CTF topic exposure counts -- Web Exploitation 9, Cryptography 9, Binary Exploitation 6, Scripting 6, Forensics 4, Unix/Computer Systems 4, Reverse Engineering 3, Network Security 3; ML-specific topic exposure counts -- Decision trees 2, Reinforcement learning 1, Neural Networks 1, Clustering 1, Adversarial ML 1, Other 1, Multilayer perceptrons 0, Hidden Markov Models 0
Study 2: 12/15 solved Challenge 1 (LollyAI, beginner); 0/15 solved Challenge 2 (poisoning chatbot, advanced)
Study 2: 73% found the 15-minute limit sufficient for Challenge 1 vs. 53% for Challenge 2; those who felt it insufficient suggested ~25 min (Ch.1) and ~30 min (Ch.2) as ideal

Study 2 difficulty ratings (mean Likert, 1=very difficult, 5=very easy) across F1-F4 (solution, overall challenge, question language, approach formulation): Challenge 1 means ~3.93, 3.64, 3.8, 3.6; Challenge 2 means ~2.21, 2.29, 3.4, 2.8

Study 2 Fisher's exact test: H1 (educational background vs. CTF preference for AML learning, Q2 vs Q28) alpha=0.006 (significant); H2 (CTF usage history vs. CTF preference for AML learning, Q7 vs Q28) alpha=0.6 (not significant)
